Oil revenues support government service delivery. Parliament and judiciary
function within authoritarian constraints. Security services (DRS under
General Toufik Mediène) maintain significant institutional power independent
of formal presidential authority.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring protests in January-February 2011 — government responds with
emergency law lifting (February 2011, in place since 1992), economic
concessions, and mild reform promises. Despite regional upheaval, Algerian
institutions remain stable. Government's capacity to make quick concessions
reflects institutional flexibility. Emergency law lifted but security
apparatus controls maintained through other legal mechanisms.

2012.assessment:
Constitutional amendments (February 2012) bring limited reforms. New
political parties law creates opening for multi-party participation.
Parliamentary elections (May 2012) — opposition parties participate.
FLN/RND still dominate but process more pluralistic. Institutions
maintaining function under Bouteflika's consolidated presidency.

2013.assessment:
Bouteflika's April 2013 stroke begins institutional degradation — president
increasingly incapacitated, governance delegated to informal networks.
In Amenas crisis response tested security institutions (effective response).
Formal institutions continue functioning but with increasing opaqueness
in decision-making as president's capacity declines.

2014.assessment:
Bouteflika wins fourth term (April 2014 election) despite wheelchair-bound
health. Presidential re-election through formal electoral process but
widely seen as facade. Governance effectively managed by informal clique
— Saïd Bouteflika (brother), DRS (Toufik), Army Chief Gaid Salah.
Formal institutions functioning but real decision-making opaque.
Classified as severely_degraded due to presidential incapacity.

2015.assessment:
Continued governance through informal networks around incapacitated president.
Fall of oil prices (2014-2015) creates fiscal stress requiring institutional
response — budget cuts, subsidy reductions. Gaid Salah begins consolidating
military authority as DRS power checked. Shadow governance deepening.

2016.assessment:
Constitutional amendments (February 2016) passed — Tamazight becomes
official language, term limits restored (for future presidents), other
reforms. DRS restructured by Gaid Salah — General Toufik (Mohammed
Mediène) removed September 2015. Military increasingly dominant over
intelligence services. Formal institutions operating but power increasingly
concentrated in military-presidential clique.

2017.assessment:
Shadow governance continues. Bouteflika largely absent from public life.
Parliamentary elections (May 2017) — FLN/RND maintain majority through
electoral manipulation. Gaid Salah's military authority increasingly dominant.
Saïd Bouteflika inner circle managing presidential decisions. Fiscal
austerity management through formal budget channels.

2018.assessment:
Pre-Hirak year. Institutions functioning formally but decision-making
increasingly captured by Bouteflika inner circle (Saïd Bouteflika, Toufik
former DRS). Growing popular discontent with opaque governance. Economic
management institutions under pressure from sustained low oil prices.

2019.assessment:
Hirak protests (February-April 2019) force Bouteflika resignation (April 2).
Constitutional Council process activates — Abdelkader Bensalah serves as
interim president. Gaid Salah manages political transition through constitutional
procedures. Multiple elections postponed as Hirak continues demanding systemic
reform. Constitutional institutions invoked to manage transition. Regime change
managed through formal institutional processes rather than breakdown.
Tebboune elected December 2019.

2020.assessment:
Post-Hirak institutional consolidation. Tebboune presidency from January 2020.
COVID-19 pandemic — emergency measures. New constitution referendum November 2020
(23% turnout — very low). Institutions consolidating under new military-backed
government. DRS rebuilt as DSI. Parliament functioning. Institutional order
restored to partially functional level from 2019 transition uncertainty.

2021.assessment:
Institutional order consolidated under Tebboune. Parliamentary elections
(June 2021) — very low turnout (23%). FLN/RND majority maintained. Security
crackdown on Hirak activists. Mass arrests of journalists and activists.
Institutions functioning within tighter authoritarian constraints than
Bouteflika era. New constitution in force.

2022.assessment:
Institutional functioning stable under Tebboune government. High oil prices
(post-Ukraine war) restore fiscal space. Morocco and Sahel security concerns
managed through security institutions. Parliamentary opposition marginalized.
Institutions partially functional but within tight authoritarian constraints.

2023.assessment:
Continued stable but constrained institutional functioning. Algeria pursues
activist foreign policy (BRICS membership sought, Sahel diplomacy, Palestinian
cause). Domestic institutions functional at service delivery level. Political
pluralism severely constrained.

2024.assessment:
Presidential election (September 2024) — Tebboune re-elected 94% (first
round) — widely disputed as neither free nor fair. Electoral institutions
function formally but outcome predetermined. Institutions partially
functional with continued military backstop.

2025.assessment:
Algeria's institutions maintain partial functionality — capable of managing
routine governance, fiscal management, and service delivery within the
authoritarian framework established by the military-political system.
The Hirak episode demonstrated capacity for managed elite transition
while preserving core institutional architecture.

2004.assessment:
Post-civil war Algeria. The Black Decade (1991-2002) claimed approximately
100,000-200,000 lives; by 2004 violence sharply reduced from civil war peaks.
Residual GSPC (Group Salafiste pour la Prédication et le Combat) insurgency
responsible for occasional ambushes and bombings in Kabylie mountains and
Boumerdes region. Estimated 200-400 conflict deaths in 2004 — dramatically
lower than 1990s when 10,000-30,000 died annually.

2005.assessment:
Continued decline in political violence. Charter for Peace and National
Reconciliation (September 2005 referendum) — amnesty for most Islamist
fighters prompts significant demobilization. GSPC holdouts continue
attacks but numbers declining. Approximately 200-300 conflict deaths.
Violence increasingly concentrated in Boumerdes and Tizi Ouzou wilayas.

2006.assessment:
Declining violence. GSPC leader Droukdel begins AQ ideology shift.
September 2006 al-Zawahiri announcement of GSPC merger with al-Qaeda.
Despite ideological escalation, actual violence declining as demobilization
continues. Approximately 150-250 conflict deaths. Mountain zone ambushes
primary tactic.

2007.assessment:
AQIM formally designated January 2007. Spike in high-profile attacks:
April 2007 simultaneous Algiers bombings targeting government headquarters
and police commissariat (33+ killed). December 2007 bombings of UN offices
and Constitutional Council (41 killed including 17 UN staff). Deadliest
year since late 1990s. Approximately 300-500 conflict deaths in 2007.

2008.assessment:
AQIM activity continues but beginning to shift focus to Sahel/kidnapping
operations. Algiers no longer primary target; operations move to mountain
zones and southern Algeria. Security forces intensify counterterrorism
operations. Approximately 200-350 conflict deaths.

2009.assessment:
Declining AQIM violence in Algeria as group focuses on Sahel kidnapping
operations for revenue. Mountain zone insurgency continuing but at reduced
intensity. Security forces maintain pressure. Approximately 150-250 deaths.

2010.assessment:
Further decline in AQIM violence in Algeria. Group increasingly focused
on trans-Saharan operations and kidnapping in Mali/Mauritania/Niger.
Algerian mountain insurgency at lowest level since early 1990s conflict.
Approximately 100-200 deaths — approaching minimal threshold.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring year — Algeria avoids major protests and violence. Small
demonstrations suppressed with limited violence. AQIM activity in Algeria
very low. Libya conflict (neighboring) creates refugee flows and arms
proliferation concern. Approximately 80-120 conflict deaths — approaching
minimal level from previously low baseline.

2012.assessment:
Low-level AQIM activity in Algeria. Mali crisis (AQIM/Tuareg control of
northern Mali from March 2012) creates southern border security concern
but Algeria does not intervene militarily. Domestic AQIM activity minimal.
Parliamentary elections (May 2012) without significant violence.

2013.assessment:
In Amenas gas facility crisis (January 2013) — Belmokhtar-led attack.
38 hostages killed (10 Algerian, 28 foreign) plus 29 attackers in military
assault. This is the dominant political violence event of 2013. French
Operation Serval in Mali (January 2013) eliminates AQIM Mali bases.
Overall death count elevated by In Amenas but not systemic insurgency.
Approximately 150-250 deaths including In Amenas casualties.

2014.assessment:
Post-In Amenas heightened security reduces AQIM capability inside Algeria.
Jund al-Khilafa (Algeria ISIS affiliate) splits from AQIM. September 2014
Jund al-Khilafa beheads French tourist Herve Gourdel. Overall deaths
relatively low. Bouteflika wins fourth term (April 2014) election despite
health concerns — no political violence during election.

2015.assessment:
Very low political violence. Jund al-Khilafa defeated by security forces.
AQIM Algeria presence reduced to small isolated cells in Kabylie mountains.
Security forces conducting systematic elimination operations. Annual conflict
deaths approximately 60-100. Algeria increasingly stable.

2016.assessment:
Very low and declining violence. AQIM in Algeria reduced to small
remnant groups in mountain zones. Security forces operations continuing.
Political violence primarily occasional ambushes on security patrols.
Annual deaths approximately 50-80.

2017.assessment:
Minimal political violence. AQIM Algeria at historic low after decades
of conflict. Most activity confined to isolated cells making occasional
attacks. Security services effective at penetration and neutralization.
Annual deaths approximately 40-60.

2018.assessment:
Near-minimal political violence. AQIM insurgency in Algeria effectively
neutralized as organized force. Individual cells may survive but unable
to mount significant operations. Algeria's security services maintain
intensive counterterrorism operations. Annual deaths under 50.

2019.assessment:
Hirak protest movement (February onward) — massive, peaceful pro-democracy
demonstrations. No violent state crackdown on protests. Bouteflika resigns
April 2019. Security forces do not use lethal force against protesters.
AQIM insurgency violence near zero. Political transition via institutional
process — extremely low political violence despite major political change.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 stops Hirak protests (March 2020). Government uses pandemic
to consolidate post-Bouteflika order. Political prisoners arrested but
no lethal crackdown. AQIM insurgency near zero. Some protest-related
arrests but no deaths in political violence. Very low annual deaths.

2021.assessment:
Very low political violence. Hirak resumes briefly in early 2021 before
suppression through mass arrests (1,500+ arrested). Tizi Ouzou wildfires
(August 2021) and killing of Djamel Ben Ismail by mob — politically charged
but limited violent deaths. AQIM insurgency near zero.

2022.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Morocco diplomatic tensions increase but no
violence. Algeria-Morocco border closed. Hirak movement effectively
suppressed. AQIM absent from Algerian territory as effective force.
Annual deaths fewer than 30 — historically low for Algeria.

2023.assessment:
Historically minimal political violence. AQIM effectively neutralized
in Algeria. No significant protest movement. Sahel instability (Niger
coup July 2023) does not spill into Algeria. Political prisoners held
but no lethal repression events.

2024.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Presidential election (September 2024) —
Tebboune re-elected amid low turnout. No election-related violence.
AQIM dormant inside Algeria. No major protest movement activity.
Algeria historically low violence context persists.

2025.assessment:
Expected to remain at minimal political violence level. Algeria's post-civil
war security apparatus maintains effective internal control. AQIM no longer
an effective presence in Algerian territory. Sahel instability a continuing
concern but limited spillover into Algeria itself.

2004.assessment:
Algerian government controls all major cities, populated areas, and
economic centers. Civil war of 1990s effectively ended — national
reconciliation process ongoing. Residual GSPC (Group Salafiste pour la
Prédication et le Combat) insurgency active in Kabylie mountains and
southern Saharan zones. Less than 5% of territory has significant
insurgent activity. No major population centers contested. Government
has near-full territorial control.

sources.citation: US State Department Algeria Country Report 2004

2005.assessment:
Near-full government territorial control. Charter for Peace and National
Reconciliation referendum (September 2005) part of post-civil war
consolidation. GSPC activity declining from 1990s peaks. Remote areas
in Sahara (Tamanrasset, Illizi) and mountain areas (Boumerdes, Tizi Ouzou)
subject to occasional GSPC operations but no sustained territorial control
by non-state actors.

2006.assessment:
Government maintains strong territorial control. GSPC begins pivot toward
AQ ideology and trans-Saharan operations. September 2006 Ayman al-Zawahiri
announces GSPC merger with al-Qaeda (formalized as AQIM in January 2007).
This ideological shift does not substantially change territorial control
picture — insurgency remains geographically confined.

2007.assessment:
AQIM (Al-Qaeda in the Islamic Maghreb) formally named in January 2007.
High-profile attacks: April 2007 Algiers bombings (government headquarters,
police station — 33 killed). December 2007 UNDP and Constitutional Council
bombings. Despite spectacular attacks, government control of territory
unchanged — attacks are strikes against fixed targets not territorial
contestation.

2008.assessment:
Government controls all population centers and key infrastructure.
AQIM activity shifts increasingly toward Sahel region — Mali, Mauritania,
Niger — while conducting kidnappings and attacks in Algeria's south.
Boumerdes and Kabylie regions see continued but declining insurgency.
Military operations reduce AQIM presence in northern Algeria.

sources.citation: US State Department Algeria Country Report 2008

2009.assessment:
Government territorial control essentially unchallenged in populated Algeria.
AQIM operates primarily in Mali, Mauritania, and Niger with limited cross-border
raids. Southern Algerian Sahara (approximately 80% of territory by area but
very sparsely populated) sees AQIM transit routes but no sustained presence.
Military maintains control of key southern installations.

2010.assessment:
Strong government territorial control. AQIM kidnapping campaign in Sahel
generating significant ransom revenue. Algerian security forces maintain
effective counterterrorism pressure in northern Algeria. Tizi Ouzou and
Boumerdes still see occasional AQIM attacks but declining in frequency.

sources.citation: US State Department Algeria Country Report 2010

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring regional context. Algeria avoids large-scale domestic uprising
due to memory of 1990s civil war and early government concessions. Libya
conflict (neighboring state) creates border security concerns. AQIM
activity in south continues but government maintains territorial control
throughout Algeria. No significant population centers contested.

2012.assessment:
Government maintains full territorial control in populated Algeria.
Mali coup (March 2012) and AQIM/Tuareg control of northern Mali creates
security concern on Algeria's southern border. Algeria maintains security
perimeter but does not intervene militarily in Mali. Border regions
see increased AQIM and smuggling activity.

2013.assessment:
January 2013 In Amenas gas facility hostage crisis — AQIM-linked Mokhtar
Belmokhtar leads attack on BP/Statoil facility, taking hundreds hostage;
38 hostages and 29 militants killed in Algerian military assault. This
spectacular attack does not represent territorial control loss but exposes
vulnerability of southern energy infrastructure. Government maintains all
territorial control. French intervention in Mali (Operation Serval January
2013) reduces AQIM Sahel threat.

2014.assessment:
Government maintains near-full territorial control. ISIS emerges in Iraq/Syria;
some Algerian fighters join ISIS. Jund al-Khilafa splinter group (formerly AQIM)
pledges to ISIS — beheads French tourist Herve Gourdel (September 2014).
This does not represent significant territorial challenge. Security forces
intensify operations in Boumerdes/Kabylie mountains.

sources.citation: US State Department Algeria Country Reports on Terrorism 2014

2015.assessment:
Government territorial control robust. AQIM and Jund al-Khilafa/ISIS
affiliates remain active in remote mountain and southern regions but
declining in intensity. Bouteflika's fourth term (re-elected 2014 despite
health) stable but increasingly autocratic. No territorial challenges to
central government authority.

2016.assessment:
Continued near-full government territorial control. Constitutional amendments
(February 2016) consolidate authoritarian governance. Residual AQIM activity
in mountain zones declining. Libya border security concern as Libyan conflict
spills arms and fighters into region. Government increasing southern border
security with new fortifications.

sources.citation: US State Department Algeria Country Report 2016

2017.assessment:
Full government territorial control. AQIM in Algeria reduced to very small
numbers and geographically isolated. ISIS defeated in Iraq/Syria; some
returnees concern. Algeria's security services maintain intensive surveillance
of potential extremist networks. No areas outside government control.

2018.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Bouteflika health increasingly incapacitating
(stroke 2013) but institutional control maintained by military and DRS intelligence
apparatus. No territorial challenges. Growing social discontent with economic
stagnation but no organized territorial challenge to state authority.

2019.assessment:
Hirak protest movement (February-April 2019) — massive peaceful protests
against Bouteflika fifth term bid. Army chief of staff Gaid Salah forces
Bouteflika resignation (April 2019). Despite political upheaval, government
maintains full territorial control throughout. Protests were nonviolent
and posed no territorial challenge. Regime change is elite-managed not
insurgent-driven. Tebboune elected December 2019.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 pandemic halts Hirak protests (March 2020). Tebboune government
uses pandemic to consolidate. Government maintains full territorial control.
New constitution approved November 2020 referendum (low turnout). No
insurgent activity of significance.

2021.assessment:
Government maintains full territorial control. Hirak resumes briefly
in early 2021 but suppressed through mass arrests. Tizi Ouzou wildfires
(August 2021) — alleged government neglect fuels Kabyle discontent.
No insurgent or armed group territorial challenge. Constitutional order
maintained by military-dominated government.

2022.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Algeria deploys military to southern
borders amid Sahel instability (Mali coup 2021, Burkina Faso coup 2022).
Wagner Group Russian presence in Mali concerns Algeria. But Algerian
territory fully under government control with no internal armed challenge.

2023.assessment:
Government maintains full territorial control. Sahel instability continuing
with coups in Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger (July 2023). Algeria's border security
enhanced. Niger coup particularly concerning given shared border. But Algerian
territory itself fully under government control.

2024.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Presidential election September 2024 —
Tebboune re-elected in first round (94% claimed — widely questioned).
No insurgent challenge to government control. Southern borders monitored
amid continued Sahel instability. AQIM no longer an effective force in
Algerian territory.

2025.assessment:
Algeria maintains full government territorial control. The security
apparatus built during the 1990s civil war remains the most capable
in North Africa. No insurgent, separatist, or armed political movement
poses a territorial challenge. Southern borders monitored but Algerian
territory secure.

2004.assessment:
Algeria was not included in the Open Budget Survey in 2004 (OBS began
in 2006). Based on Freedom House and IMF Article IV assessments, budget
transparency was scant. The Algerian budget process was dominated by the
executive and the hydrocarbon sector (Sonatrach revenues largely off-budget
or opaque). Parliament received budget documents but scrutiny was limited.
Pre-budget statements not published. Citizens had minimal access to fiscal
information. Scant range.

2005.assessment:
No OBS survey. Budget transparency minimal. Oil boom revenues flowing
through Fonds de Régulation des Recettes (FRR) with limited public
reporting. Executive-dominated budget process. Some improvement in
publishing basic budget documents post-2005 but far below international
standards. Scant range.

2006.assessment:
First OBS survey: Algeria scored 6/100 — scant budget transparency.
Among the lowest scores globally. The government published an executive
budget proposal but citizens budget, mid-year review, and audit reports
were not publicly available. Hydrocarbon revenues (bulk of state income)
particularly opaque. FRR (sovereign wealth fund) reporting minimal.
Parliament budget scrutiny limited. Scant range.

2007.assessment:
No new OBS survey in 2007 (biennial surveys). Transparency estimated
consistent with 2006 score of 6/100. No significant reforms to budget
transparency in 2007. Oil boom continues but no improvement in public
reporting of FRR or hydrocarbon revenues. Scant range.

2008.assessment:
OBS 2008: Algeria scored 13/100 — improvement from 6 but still scant.
Some additional budget documents being published. Executive budget proposal
available. But audit reports, citizens budgets, and mid-year reviews
remain inaccessible. Hydrocarbon revenue reporting still opaque. FRR
grew to $77 billion but with minimal public accountability. Scant range.

2009.assessment:
No new OBS survey. Transparency maintained at scant level (~13).
Finance Law 2009 with 49/51 rule passed with limited public consultation.
Budget process remained executive-controlled. No improvement in FRR
transparency despite growing fund. Scant range.

2010.assessment:
OBS 2010: Algeria scored 13/100 — unchanged from 2008. Budget
transparency stagnant. Some additional information published online
but still far below minimally adequate. Hydrocarbon revenues remain
the weakest transparency area. Algeria's FRR was among Africa's largest
but public had virtually no information on its management. Scant range.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring year. Emergency law lifted but no budget transparency reforms.
Government increased social spending to quell unrest (salary increases,
housing programs) but these expenditures were announced without budget
transparency process. FRR spending accelerated. Budget transparency
remained scant by all measures.

2012.assessment:
OBS 2012: Algeria scored 10/100 — slight decline from 13. Budget
transparency worsened. Despite high oil revenues, government publishing
fewer budget documents than in 2008-2010. Citizens budget not produced.
Audit institution (Cour des Comptes) not fully independent or publishing
reports. IMF called for improved fiscal reporting. Scant range.

2013.assessment:
No new OBS survey. Transparency maintained at scant level (~10).
Bouteflika health crisis (April 2013 stroke) created information vacuum
on government decision-making and budget policy. No transparency
improvements. Scant range.

2014.assessment:
OBS 2015 (covering 2014 period): Algeria scored 10/100. Budget
transparency unchanged at scant. Oil price collapse H2 2014 drove
emergency budget adjustments but these were managed with minimal
transparency. Fourth-term Bouteflika government with incapacitated
president operating through shadow governance — budget process even
less accountable. Scant range.

2015.assessment:
OBS 2015: Algeria scored 10/100. Austerity budget implemented following
oil price crash but with minimal public consultation or transparency.
Citizens budget not produced. Legislature approval of FRR drawdowns
was perfunctory. Among the lowest budget transparency scores in MENA.
Scant range.

2016.assessment:
No new OBS survey. Transparency estimate ~10. Constitutional reforms passed
but did not include budget transparency provisions. FRR almost depleted by
end of 2016. Monetary financing (printing money) initiated without transparent
process. Scant range.

2017.assessment:
OBS 2017: Algeria scored 10/100. FRR exhausted October 2017 — monetary
financing of deficit begins. This major fiscal policy shift occurred with
virtually no public debate or transparency. IMF expressed concern about
lack of fiscal transparency. Among the worst performers in the MENA region
and globally. Scant range.

2018.assessment:
No new OBS survey. Transparency estimate ~10. Monetary financing
continues. Some additional IMF pressure for FSAP (Financial Sector
Assessment Program) but Algeria resisted. Budget documents technically
submitted to Parliament but with minimal public disclosure. Scant range.

2019.assessment:
OBS 2019: Algeria scored 10/100. Hirak protests partly driven by concerns
about government opacity and corruption. Despite massive public pressure,
no budget transparency reforms during the regime transition year. Military-
managed transition did not prioritize fiscal transparency. Scant range.

2020.assessment:
No new OBS survey. COVID-19 emergency spending through non-transparent
mechanisms. New constitution November 2020 did not include budget
transparency provisions. Tebboune government inheriting same opaque
fiscal structures. Scant range.

2021.assessment:
OBS 2021: Algeria scored 15/100 — modest improvement from 10 but still
scant. Some additional budget documents now available online. Citizens
budget still not produced. Audit reports still not widely distributed.
Hydrocarbon revenue transparency improved marginally with EITI involvement
but fiscal transparency overall remains very weak. Scant range.

2022.assessment:
No new OBS survey. Transparency estimate ~15. Ukraine war windfall revenues
flowing in but with limited transparency. New Investment Law 2022 focused
on FDI rather than fiscal transparency. Some additional reporting of
hydrocarbon revenues but overall budget transparency structure unchanged.
Scant range.

2023.assessment:
OBS 2023: Algeria scored 17/100 — marginal improvement continuing.
Still scant. Some additional fiscal documents published online.
Pre-budget statement still not produced. Citizens budget not available.
Oversight by legislature and SAI (Supreme Audit Institution) limited.
Algeria among MENA countries with weakest budget transparency despite
significant hydrocarbon revenues. Scant range.

2024.assessment:
OBS 2024 not yet published. Estimated at ~17 based on 2023 score.
Tebboune re-election September 2024. No announced reforms to budget
transparency framework. Some digitization of government services continuing.
Scant range.

2025.assessment:
Algeria expected to remain in scant range for budget transparency in 2025.
No announced structural reforms. The persistent pattern of 6-17/100 OBI
scores over 2006-2023 suggests no imminent breakthrough. Hydrocarbon
revenue opacity remains the structural obstacle to meaningful fiscal
transparency improvement.

2004.assessment:
Algeria not yet an EITI member in 2004. EITI was founded in 2002 and
Algeria did not join the early adopters. Sonatrach (state hydrocarbon
company) revenue reporting was opaque — hydrocarbon revenues were the
dominant source of government income (approximately 97% of export earnings
and 60%+ of government revenues) but disclosed only partially in
government budget documents. FRR (Fonds de Régulation des Recettes)
established 2000 to accumulate hydrocarbon windfall revenue — reported
minimally. No independent verification of extractive revenues. No
membership, minimal disclosure range.

sources.citation: EITI global list of implementing countries 2004
IMF Algeria Article IV 2004 — fiscal transparency concerns

2005.assessment:
Algeria not EITI member. Oil boom year — Sonatrach revenues at record
levels but disclosure minimal. IMF called for improved fiscal reporting
of hydrocarbon revenues. No independent audit of Sonatrach. Minimal
disclosure.

2006.assessment:
Algeria not EITI member. Some African neighbors (Nigeria, Ghana) joining
EITI but Algeria did not follow. Hydrocarbon Law amendment (requiring
Sonatrach 51% ownership) enacted — further restricting IOC transparency
vis-a-vis public disclosure. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2007.assessment:
Algeria not EITI member. Some discussions about Algeria potentially
joining but no formal candidacy. AQIM attacks on Sonatrach assets
(In Amenas precursor events) not triggering transparency measures.
Minimal disclosure.

2008.assessment:
Algeria not EITI member despite FDI concerns (investors calling for
transparency). Oil boom at peak — FRR approaching $77 billion but
with minimal public reporting. IMF Article IV 2008 specifically
recommended Algeria join EITI to improve hydrocarbon revenue transparency.
No membership, minimal disclosure.

2009.assessment:
Algeria not EITI member. 49/51 rule (Finance Law 2009) further
complicated investment transparency. Oil price crash H2 2008 reduced
revenues but still no EITI application. Minimal disclosure.

2010.assessment:
Algeria not EITI member. Sonatrach corruption scandal (January 2010) —
multiple senior Sonatrach executives arrested for corruption in contracts.
This major scandal highlighted opacity in extractive sector management.
Paradoxically did not immediately trigger EITI application but increased
awareness of need for transparency. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2011.assessment:
Algeria not EITI member. Arab Spring — some reform promises but extractive
transparency not among them. Sonatrach corruption trial beginning. IMF
repeatedly called for extractive revenue transparency. EU-Algeria partnership
discussions included governance aspects. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2012.assessment:
Algeria begins EITI candidacy process in 2012. The government submitted
a letter of intent to the EITI Board in 2012, signaling intent to become
an implementing country. This was driven partly by IMF pressure, partly
by post-Sonatrach scandal cleanup, and partly by desire to attract foreign
investment. The formal candidacy status was granted by the EITI Board in
October 2013. In 2012: EITI candidate application in progress but not
yet formally designated. Minimal disclosure but process beginning.

2013.assessment:
Algeria formally designated as EITI Candidate Country by the EITI Board
in October 2013. This is a significant step — candidate countries must
establish a multi-stakeholder group (MSG) including government, companies,
and civil society, and produce the first EITI Report within 18 months.
Algeria's MSG established. In Amenas attack (January 2013) initially
slowed process but candidacy proceeded. Candidate range.

sources.citation: EITI Board Decision — Algeria Candidate October 2013

2014.assessment:
Algeria continues EITI candidacy. Multi-stakeholder group (MSG) operational.
Work on first EITI Report covering fiscal year 2012 data commenced. Sonatrach
and oil company revenue data being reconciled. Technical challenges in
reconciling company payments to government receipts. Candidate range — first
report not yet published.

2015.assessment:
Algeria publishes its first EITI Report (covering FY 2012 data) in 2015.
This report reconciled Sonatrach and IOC payments to government revenues.
The EITI Board assessed Algeria's progress. Algeria completed candidacy
requirements and was validated. Algeria achieved EITI Compliant status
in 2015 (validated as meeting EITI Standard). This was a significant
achievement for extractive sector transparency. Compliant range.

2016.assessment:
Algeria maintains EITI Compliant status. Second EITI Report published
(FY 2013 data). EITI Standard updated globally in 2016 — Algeria
required to adapt reports to new standard. MSG continued. Corrective
actions identified in validation process being addressed. Compliant
with corrective actions range.

2017.assessment:
Algeria continues publishing annual EITI Reports. Third EITI Report
(FY 2014 data) published. FRR exhausted in October 2017 — the EITI
reporting of hydrocarbon revenues provided some visibility into this
fiscal change. EITI Compliant status maintained. Some corrective actions
ongoing. Compliant with corrective actions range.

2018.assessment:
Algeria maintains EITI Compliance. Annual reports continue. EITI
2018 Standard requires more comprehensive beneficial ownership and
contract transparency — Algeria beginning to address these requirements.
Sonatrach revenue data published in EITI reports with reconciliation.
Some areas still limited (beneficial ownership incomplete). Compliant
with corrective actions range.

2019.assessment:
Hirak year. Algeria maintained EITI Compliance through political transition.
EITI reporting continued. Algeria published EITI Report covering FY 2017.
The political transition (Bouteflika resignation April 2019) did not disrupt
EITI reporting process. EITI compliance maintained as independent technical
process from political upheaval. Compliant with corrective actions range.

2020.assessment:
Algeria maintains EITI Compliance. COVID-19 impacted some EITI processes
globally (report delays). Algeria's EITI Secretariat continued operations.
New EITI Report published. Tebboune government committed to EITI process.
Revenue data for hydrocarbon sector disclosed in reports. Compliant range.

2021.assessment:
Algeria underwent EITI Validation process in 2021. EITI Validation
assesses compliance with the EITI Standard. Algeria received a "meaningful
progress" validation outcome in recent years. Reports published annually.
Contract transparency and beneficial ownership disclosure improving but
still some gaps. Compliant meaningful range.

2022.assessment:
Algeria maintains EITI Compliant Meaningful status. Ukraine war drives
European interest in Algerian gas — European partners calling for more
transparency in Algerian energy sector. EITI framework provides some
visibility. Annual EITI Report covering FY 2020 published. Compliant
meaningful range.

2023.assessment:
Algeria maintains EITI compliance. Annual EITI Reports continue to
provide hydrocarbon revenue reconciliation data. Sonatrach and IOC
payments to government reconciled. Some progress on beneficial ownership
disclosure. Contract transparency improving. Compliant meaningful range.

2024.assessment:
Algeria expected to maintain EITI Compliant status in 2024. Tebboune
re-elected September 2024 — continued commitment to EITI process expected.
Annual EITI Report for FY 2022 or FY 2023 due. Partial 2024 data.
Compliant meaningful range.

2025.assessment:
Algeria expected to continue EITI compliance in 2025. The EITI framework
has been maintained since 2013 candidacy and 2015 compliance — a decade
of commitment to extractive sector transparency. This is the area of
strongest transparency performance for Algeria, contrasting with weak
budget, press, and legal transparency. Compliant meaningful projected.

2004.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index did not cover Algeria until 2012. Estimated from
World Bank Doing Business, Freedom House, and US DoS Rule of Law assessments.
Algeria's legal framework was based on French civil law tradition with
significant authoritarian modifications. Laws published in Official Gazette
(Journal Officiel) but access limited. Regulations often issued via
executive decrees with minimal advance notice. Judiciary not independent —
courts subject to executive influence particularly in politically sensitive cases.
Enforcement of contract law unpredictable. Business environment surveys
consistently rated Algeria as difficult for legal predictability. Difficult
and arbitrary enforcement range.

2005.assessment:
Legal transparency remained at difficult/arbitrary enforcement level.
Some commercial law improvements related to EU-Algeria Association Agreement
implementation (in force September 2005). New commercial code provisions
improving business registration somewhat. But judicial independence minimal.
Administrative regulations often opaque. Difficult range.

2006.assessment:
Some commercial law reforms — World Bank Doing Business 2006 showed
marginal improvement in contract enforcement procedures. But administrative
transparency and judicial independence remain weak. Official Gazette
publishing laws and decrees but regulatory guidance unclear. Difficult range.

2007.assessment:
Legal transparency largely unchanged. Hydrocarbon Law amendments requiring
Sonatrach 51% in all upstream contracts — retroactive changes creating
legal uncertainty for investors. Regulatory framework for FDI becoming
more restrictive and less predictable. Difficult range.

2008.assessment:
Minimal improvement in legal transparency. Third term enabled through
constitutional amendment — rule by decree expanded. Finance Law 2009
(enacted 2008) with 49/51 rule — major new investment regulation with
retroactive effect concerns. WB Doing Business ranked Algeria 132nd/183
for ease of doing business, reflecting poor legal predictability.
Difficult range.

2009.assessment:
Finance Law 2009 enacted — 49/51 rule for all foreign investment. This
law introduced with minimal legal consultation or regulatory impact
assessment. Retroactive uncertainty for existing foreign investments.
Some investors found new regulatory requirements conflicted with existing
contracts. Legal transparency worsening for investment law. Difficult range.

2010.assessment:
Legal environment for business and civil society deteriorating. Emergency
law still in effect. Legal framework for civil society organizations
restrictive (Law 90-31 on associations). Courts used to suppress political
opponents. Some commercial law improvements in WB Doing Business reforms
but judicial independence nil. Difficult range.

2011.assessment:
Emergency law lifted February 2011 — significant legal change. But
emergency law replaced by other provisions (anti-terrorism law still
broadly applicable). New Law on Political Parties, Associations
(promised) but restricted in practice. Commercial courts functioning
but unpredictable. Difficult range maintained.

2012.assessment:
First WJP coverage of Algeria. Open government score 0.36/1.00 (36/100).
This placed Algeria in the difficult/arbitrary enforcement category with
some acknowledged legal publication. Algeria ranked below global
and regional averages. Laws published in Official Gazette but regulatory
implementation opaque. Judicial independence sub-score particularly low.
Some improvement from private TV channel opening. Difficult enforcement range.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2012 — Algeria

2013.assessment:
WJP score slightly declined (~0.35). Bouteflika stroke (April 2013) —
governance vacuum created opacity in government decision-making. Regulatory
changes enacted without normal procedure. Legal framework for investment
becoming more restrictive and less transparent. Difficult enforcement range.

2014.assessment:
WJP open government score approximately 0.34/1.00. Fourth-term election
with incapacitated president — regulatory decisions increasingly made
through shadow governance. WB Doing Business ranked Algeria 152nd/189.
Difficulty of registering and enforcing contracts high. Partial significant
gaps/difficult enforcement.

sources.citation: World Bank Doing Business 2015 — Algeria ranked 152nd/189

2015.assessment:
WJP score approximately 0.33/1.00. Austerity regulatory changes (import
restriction policies, currency controls) introduced with limited legal
process. New laws enacted via presidential ordinances bypassing normal
legislative process. Shadow governance intensifying. Difficult range.

2016.assessment:
Constitutional reform 2016 — new constitution enacted but legal framework
for rights not meaningfully improved. WJP score approximately 0.33/1.00.
Anti-terrorism law provisions broadly used against peaceful activists.
Labor code reforms reducing worker protections. WB Doing Business Algeria
ranked 163rd/190 (significant deterioration). Difficult range.

2017.assessment:
WJP score approximately 0.32/1.00. Monetary financing policy introduced
without transparent legal framework — decree-based changes to Banking Law.
Penal Code Article 144 and related provisions increasingly used. Difficult range.

2018.assessment:
WJP score approximately 0.31/1.00 — declining trend. Pre-Hirak legal
environment increasingly restrictive. Broad use of anti-terrorism and
defamation provisions against civil society. WB Doing Business Algeria
ranked 157th/190. Investment laws complex and non-transparent. Difficult range.

2019.assessment:
Hirak year. WJP score approximately 0.31/1.00. Hirak protesters calling
for rule of law and end to impunity. Military used legal mechanisms
(anti-terrorism charges, defamation) to prosecute Hirak leaders and
former regime figures (Saïd Bouteflika, Gaid Salah opponents). Legal
framework used instrumentally. WB Doing Business 157th/190. Difficult range.

2020.assessment:
WJP score approximately 0.30/1.00. Post-Hirak crackdown — anti-terrorism
and other Penal Code provisions used extensively against civil society.
New Penal Code amendments (Ordinance 20-06) expanding criminalization of
online speech. Constitution November 2020 — some new provisions for judicial
independence on paper but no practical change. Difficult range.

2021.assessment:
WJP score approximately 0.30/1.00. Mass arrests of activists and journalists
using various legal provisions. Investment Law reformed (2021 Finance Law)
with some simplification of FDI regulations. New Investment Code 2022
preparation. Some WB Doing Business improvement (Algeria 157th but with
some sub-indicator progress). Difficult range.

2022.assessment:
WJP score approximately 0.31/1.00 — marginal improvement. New Investment
Code (Law 22-18) passed in July 2022 — significant investment law reform
simplifying procedures and partially revising 49/51 rule. Some legal
transparency improvement for investment. But civil liberties legal framework
unchanged. Difficult range.

2023.assessment:
WJP score approximately 0.32/1.00 — slight improvement continuing.
Investment Code 2022 implemented. Some digitization of administrative
procedures improving legal accessibility. But journalist sentencing
(Ihsane El Kadi) and broad use of anti-terrorism law against activists
continue. Overall difficult enforcement range.

2024.assessment:
WJP 2024 index not yet published. Estimated at ~0.32 based on 2023.
Tebboune re-election September 2024 — no announced legal transparency
reforms. Criminal law use against civil society continues. Some improvement
in administrative digitization and investment law. Difficult range.

2025.assessment:
Algeria expected to remain in difficult/arbitrary enforcement range
in 2025. The structural features — non-independent judiciary, broad
criminal provisions against speech, inconsistent regulatory enforcement,
weak FOI framework — are unlikely to change significantly without
political will for judicial reform. WJP 2025 data not yet available.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index projections

2004.assessment:
Press freedom very serious in 2004. Algeria ranked 126th out of 167 countries
by RSF. The state maintained dominant control of broadcast media (ENTV, Radio
Algérienne). Private print press existed but faced legal harassment, defamation
suits, and advertising boycotts by state-owned enterprises. Journalists covering
security topics faced particular risks. The Press Code required prior authorization.
Some independent newspapers (El Watan, Le Quotidien d'Oran) operated with
significant self-censorship. Post-civil war trauma meant journalists avoided
sensitive topics. Very serious range.

2005.assessment:
Press freedom remained very serious. Slight improvement in RSF ranking
(121st). Private print media active but heavily self-censored. Criminal
defamation laws used against journalists who criticized officials.
Some improvement in internet access though social media not yet
politically significant. State broadcast monopoly maintained. Very serious range.

2006.assessment:
Press freedom conditions worsened slightly. Algeria 126th/168 in RSF.
Several journalists imprisoned on defamation charges. Criminal defamation
laws regularly weaponized against critical coverage. GSPC/AQIM attacks
created security justifications for restricting coverage of conflict areas.
Broadcast media entirely state-controlled. Very serious range.

2007.assessment:
Press freedom unchanged at very serious level. AQIM bombings of April
and December 2007 — media coverage of security events tightly controlled.
Journalists covering Islamist groups faced intimidation from both state and
armed groups. Self-censorship on military/security topics pervasive. State
advertisng boycott of critical newspapers continued. Very serious range.

2008.assessment:
Marginal improvement in rank but conditions remain very serious.
Bouteflika term limit removal (constitutional amendment) covered by
press with significant self-censorship. Critical coverage of the
amendment was rare. Private newspapers existed but navigated narrow
space between official tolerance and harassment. Very serious range.

2009.assessment:
Press freedom deteriorated; Algeria fell to 141st/175. Third presidential
term with constitutional amendment — media coverage of elections highly
restricted. Several journalists arrested or prosecuted. Algeria one of
highest-ranked jailers of journalists in Africa according to CPJ.
Very serious to no press freedom range.

2010.assessment:
Press freedom conditions remained very serious. Internet and social media
use growing but state could monitor online activity. Criminal defamation
laws maintained. Emergency law still in effect (lifted February 2011).
Broadcast media entirely state-controlled. Very serious range.

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring: emergency law lifted February 2011. Some temporary opening
in media space as government made concessions. RSF improved Algeria's
ranking to 122nd/179 (slightly better). New private TV licenses announced
(though not immediately implemented). Some online media blossomed briefly.
But structural controls remained — criminal defamation, state broadcast
monopoly, advertising dependency on state. Very serious range.

2012.assessment:
Some improvement in press freedom as private television channels launched
(Ennahar TV, Echorouk TV, Dzair TV — 2012-2013). This was significant
opening in broadcast media that had been state monopoly. Online press
growing rapidly. RSF 125th/179. However criminal defamation maintained,
coverage of sensitive security topics restricted, and some satellite
operators blocked. Difficult range — approaching improvement.

2013.assessment:
Press conditions mixed. Private TV channels now operating but facing
regulatory pressure and ownership links to business elite close to
power. In Amenas attack (January 2013) — media coverage controlled.
Bouteflika stroke (April 2013) major information blackout; media
forbidden from discussing president's health in detail. Criminal
defamation suits continued. Difficult range.

2014.assessment:
Bouteflika fourth term election coverage heavily managed. President
appeared in few public appearances; media obligated to cover campaign
with state-provided materials. Private TV channels continued operating
but increasingly owned by pro-government businessmen. Websites of
independent media blocked intermittently. Very serious to difficult range.

2015.assessment:
Press freedom deteriorated further as RSF ranked Algeria 129th/180.
Constitutional reform discussions in press were limited. Economic austerity
discussion restricted. Private TV channels growing but captured by
pro-government oligarchy. Several bloggers and social media users arrested.
Very serious range.

2016.assessment:
Algeria 134th/180 in RSF 2017 (covering 2016). Constitutional amendments
passed but did not improve press freedom framework. Independent news websites
growing as print declined. State used Penal Code articles and anti-terrorism
law provisions against online criticism. Journalists detained for coverage
of social protests (particularly in oil-producing regions). Very serious range.

2017.assessment:
Algeria 136th/180 in RSF 2018 (covering 2017). Journalist Mohamed Tamalt
died in prison after hunger strike (2016-2017 — he was imprisoned for
Facebook posts). This case drew international attention. Hirak precursor
social protests in Béjaïa and Tizi-Ouzou generated journalist crackdowns.
Very serious range.

sources.citation: CPJ: Mohamed Tamalt dies in prison 2017

2018.assessment:
Algeria 141st/180 in RSF 2019 (covering 2018). Growing use of Penal Code
Article 144 (offense against President) and Article 146 (insult to state
institutions) against journalists and bloggers. Private TV channels proliferated
but most owned by pro-government businessmen with self-censorship. Online
media and social media users increasingly targeted. Very serious range.

2019.assessment:
Hirak year. Despite massive peaceful protests, press freedom did not
meaningfully improve during the transition. Some independent coverage
of Hirak protests online (Radio M, Maghreb Emergent, Interlignes). But
correspondents and online journalists arrested for covering protests.
Algeria 146th/180 in RSF. The transition to Tebboune brought promises of
press freedom reform but limited implementation. Very serious range.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 Hirak suspension — protests halted. Post-Hirak crackdown
intensified. Mass arrests of journalists and activists. Algeria 146th/180.
New Penal Code amendments criminalized criticism on social media.
Radio M and Maghreb Emergent journalists arrested. New constitution
did not include press freedom protections. Very serious/no press freedom range.

2021.assessment:
Press freedom at lowest point in recent years. Algeria 146th/180 but
RSF score deteriorated significantly. Mass arrests: approximately 70+
journalists and bloggers detained or prosecuted in 2021 (RSF listed
Algeria as one of the world's largest jailers of journalists). Ihsane
El Kadi (Radio M director) arrested. Numerous bloggers and YouTubers
arrested for social media content. No press freedom range approaching.

sources.citation: RSF: Algeria one of world's top jailers of journalists 2021

2022.assessment:
Algeria improved slightly in RSF ranking (136th/180) but conditions
remained extremely serious. Some journalists released from pre-trial
detention. But Ihsane El Kadi remained detained. New criminal code
with broad provisions against online criticism. Algerian media landscape
further restricted. No press freedom range.

2023.assessment:
Ihsane El Kadi sentenced to 5 years prison (February 2023). Algeria
139th/180 in RSF. Multiple journalists and activists serving sentences
for social media posts. Private broadcasters entirely pro-government.
No independent television. Online news sites (TSA, Tout Sur l'Algérie)
operating carefully. No press freedom range.

sources.citation: RSF: Ihsane El Kadi sentenced February 2023

2024.assessment:
Tebboune re-elected September 2024. Press coverage of election tightly
controlled; independent coverage minimal. Multiple journalists still
imprisoned. RSF placed Algeria among states with no press freedom.
No announced reforms to press law or criminal defamation. No press
freedom range.

2025.assessment:
Algeria expected to remain in no press freedom range in 2025. The
structural conditions — criminal defamation laws, anti-terrorism provisions
used against journalists, state-controlled broadcast media, captured private
TV sector, imprisoned journalists — have not changed. No announced reforms.
RSF 2025 score not yet published.

2004.assessment:
Algeria's World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator score was approximately
60/100 in 2004 — moderate range. The Office National des Statistiques (ONS)
conducted regular economic and demographic surveys. GDP and national accounts
data published with some lag. However, hydrocarbon sector statistics were
partially opaque (Sonatrach data not fully integrated into national accounts).
FRR (sovereign wealth fund) reporting minimal. Population census conducted
(2008 upcoming). Methodology generally aligned with international standards
for non-hydrocarbon data. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2004

2005.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 61/100. ONS improving data quality and publication
frequency. Trade and labor market statistics improving. IMF Article IV
notes improving fiscal data quality. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2005

2006.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 63/100. Improving statistical capacity. National
accounts methodology improvement. Some new economic surveys conducted.
ONS began publishing more timely economic indicators. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2006

2007.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 64/100. Statistical capacity improving gradually.
2008 General Population and Housing Census preparation underway.
GDP data more comprehensive. Labor market surveys more regular. Some
improvement in administrative data sources. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2007

2008.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 65/100 — peak performance. General Population and
Housing Census conducted in 2008. This major data collection effort
improved population statistics significantly. GDP at record high with
good data coverage. Administrative statistics improving. Moderate to
approaching good range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2008

2009.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 63/100. Post-census statistical infrastructure
in place. IMF GDDS subscription maintained. Some gaps in reporting
frequency on social indicators. Overall moderate and sustained capacity.
Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2009

2010.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 62/100. Statistical capacity maintained at moderate
level. Unemployment statistics among the weaker areas (methodology debate
regarding informal employment). Trade statistics comprehensive for formal
sector. Fiscal data improving. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2010

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring year. Algeria maintained statistical capacity despite regional
instability. ONS continued publishing regular economic indicators. Social
spending data (post-Arab Spring concessions) not always clearly documented.
WB SCI approximately 61/100. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2011

2012.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 60/100. Statistical capacity stable. In Amenas
attack context — no impact on statistical function. GDP data complete
and comparable across years. Labor market and social surveys conducted.
Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2012

2013.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 59/100. Slight decline from 2008 peak. Bouteflika
stroke (April 2013) — some government information opacity but statistical
agency operated independently. Price statistics (CPI) methodology questioned
by some analysts due to administered prices. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2013

2014.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 58/100. Oil price crash H2 2014 — FRR drawdown
statistics not publicly reported in detail. GDP measured in both oil
and non-oil components. Statistical capacity adequate for international
comparisons. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2014

2015.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 57/100. Gradual slight decline in statistical
capacity. Austerity budget data published but FRR transparency remained
minimal. Some deterioration in frequency of labor market publications.
ONS capacity not strengthened during austerity. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2015

2016.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 56/100. Monetary financing introduced (2017)
but decision-making opacity increasing. Constitutional reform statistics
(low turnout) reported with official figures disputed by civil society.
Some indicators published with increasing lag. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2016

2017.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 55/100. FRR exhausted October 2017 but this was
not clearly announced — citizens learned gradually. Monetary financing
scale not fully reported. Some inflation data questioned (administered
prices hide true inflation). But core statistical capacity maintained.
Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2017

2018.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 55/100. Statistical capacity stable but with
concerns about monetary data completeness. ONS GDP methodology
consistent. Trade, inflation, and labor market data published regularly.
Some international comparisons complicated by currency and administered
price issues. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2018

2019.assessment:
Hirak year. WB SCI approximately 55/100. Statistical capacity maintained
through political transition. ONS continued normal operations. Economic
data (GDP, trade, inflation) published on schedule. Political transition
statistics (voter participation in November 2019 election disputed — official
figure 39.7% widely disputed) showed politicization of some data. Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2019

2020.assessment:
WB SCI approximately 53/100 (2020 was the final year of SCI — World Bank
discontinued SCI after 2020, replacing with ODIN). COVID-19 impact: some
surveys delayed or cancelled. ONS adapted to remote data collection.
GDP contraction accurately measured. Some health statistics opacity
(COVID death counts questioned). Moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator Algeria 2020 (last SCI year)

2021.assessment:
WB SCI discontinued after 2020. World Bank replaced it with ODIN (Open
Data Inventory). Algeria's ODIN score not prominently published/comparable.
Based on ONS operations and IMF assessments, statistical capacity maintained
at moderate level. GDP data comprehensive. Some improvement in timeliness
of economic data publication. Moderate range estimated.

2022.assessment:
Statistical capacity maintained at moderate level. Ukraine war windfall
revenue data published by Bank of Algeria and Finance Ministry. ONS
quarterly GDP and CPI reports continue. Some new surveys on digital
economy. Overall moderate statistical transparency despite political
repression in other areas. Moderate range estimated.

2023.assessment:
Statistical capacity continues at moderate level. IMF Article IV 2023
noted some data quality improvements. ONS conducting new enterprise
surveys. GDP deflator and price index methodology discussed with IMF.
No major deterioration. Moderate range estimated.

2024.assessment:
Statistical capacity expected to remain at moderate level. ONS continuing
regular data collection. Some improvement in digital data access. IMF
continues to engage Algeria on data quality. Partial data for 2024.
Moderate range estimated.

2025.assessment:
Algeria expected to maintain moderate statistical capacity in 2025.
The ONS has been a functioning statistical agency throughout the period
with consistent moderate capacity. No reason to expect major deterioration
or major improvement. Moderate range projected.
